summary: This paper studies multivariate time-series forecasting and identifies a specific failure mode of the dominant Direct-Mapping paradigm, in which a single learned history-to-future mapping responds with a delay around regime changes and distribution shifts, trailing the underlying dynamics and accumulating error within the switching window. The authors treat this "response lag" as a phenomenon distinct from general distribution shift and give it a mechanistic account grounded in an inertial recursion, drawing an analogy to the adaptive-expectations model from economics. To mitigate it, they propose L-Drive, whose core is a Latent-Context (L-Context) module: it normalizes the input, extracts and gates a first-order differenced (increment) signal, and integrates it through a recurrent unit to produce a time-evolving high-level context that modulates the forecast. A complementary Struct-Aided Predictor splits the fused representation into non-overlapping patches and applies patch-shared relative positional basis functions to strengthen intra-segment structure and reduce overfitting to absolute positions. A notable aspect is that L-Context is model-agnostic and can be injected into existing backbones such as DLinear, iTransformer, and PatchTST without changing their internals. The claims are supported by experiments on eight LTSF/EPF benchmarks against nine baselines, together with ablation, sensitivity, and portability studies, and dedicated lag/tail metrics (TailAUC, ExcessAUC), with emphasis on a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The clearest strength of the paper lies in its problem formulation. Explicitly isolating response lag as a distinct failure mode of Direct-Mapping models -- separate from general distribution shift -- and giving it a concrete, testable characterization is a genuinely useful contribution to the community's understanding of when and why current forecasting models break down. The synthetic Mode-1/Mode-2 illustration makes the phenomenon immediately visible and easy to reason about, and this clarity holds value independently of the specific solution proposed. On soundness, the formal analysis itself appears internally consistent: the derivation of the lag upper bound reads correctly under its stated assumptions and yields a tidy statement of the role L-Context is meant to play. The portability results are also compelling from a methodological standpoint -- obtaining consistent gains when L-Context is injected into three backbones with very different inductive biases, without touching their internals, suggests that the module captures information complementary to what those architectures already learn rather than patching a weakness specific to any one of them. The paper is generally well organized, the motivation is clear, and the release of code aids understanding.

My main reservation concerns the coupling between the theory and the models actually evaluated. The formal motivation is built on a recursive rollout in which each prediction feeds into the next step, yet essentially all of the baselines (PatchTST, iTransformer, DLinear, and the others) take a fixed lookback window and emit the entire horizon in a single forward pass, with no autoregressive loop. The recursion therefore does not describe what these models do at inference, and the lag they exhibit seems more naturally explained by the fact that post-switch observations only gradually fill the fixed lookback window, shifting the effective input distribution slowly -- a structurally different mechanism from the one formalized. Relatedly, the move from theory to architecture rests on several heuristic choices (standard normalization, 1D convolution for cross-channel alignment, gating, GRU integration) that are empirically reasonable but not derived from the theoretical desiderata, and the theoretical-implication discussion is placed after the architecture is fully specified, so it reads as post-hoc rationalization rather than design guidance. A cleaner presentation would first state what a good increment estimator must satisfy and then show that each design choice meets it. The theory also assumes that L-Context behaves as an estimator of the increment, but nothing in the training objective enforces this -- the recurrent unit is trained under the forecasting loss alone -- so the connection is assumed rather than demonstrated; a simple correlation check between L-Context outputs and ground-truth increments on held-out data would help. The scalar approximation of the model's internal state in the appendix similarly collapses a high-dimensional hidden representation to the scalar output without stating when this is valid.

The empirical validation, while extensive, has gaps that matter given the paper's central claim. Only MSE and MAE are reported, both of which are outlier-sensitive and scale-dependent -- precisely the conditions that regime switches create -- so improvements could partly reflect better handling of a few high-magnitude errors near switches rather than a broadly better forecast; scale-normalized metrics such as MASE or MAPE would be more convincing. More importantly, statistical rigor is thin: many reported gains, and several ablation margins, sit at the third decimal place (for example a 0.001 MSE difference), are averaged across horizons without variance, and are not accompanied by significance testing, so it is hard to know how many of the best-in-class results are real. The Struct-Aided Predictor is also weakly motivated relative to the rest of the paper: it is justified mainly by generic overfitting concerns rather than by the change-aware setting, and in some ablations the positional components contribute more than L-Context itself, which sits awkwardly with the paper's framing. The dedicated lag analysis raises a further concern of near-circularity, since change events are detected with the same first-order differencing operation that drives L-Context; it is shown on a single dataset, omits one baseline without explanation, and does not report the neighborhood-window or threshold settings, leaving the robustness of TailAUC and ExcessAUC to these choices unestablished.

On presentation, the paper is readable but several elements need work. Figure 2 attempts to show the entire forward pass at once and is dense to the point of being hard to follow, with overlapping notation and unlabeled intermediate tensors; the L-Context heatmap lacks a colorbar and a precise definition of the quantity being visualized, and its qualitative "bright blocks correspond to rapid change" reading is offered without quantitative support. Reproducibility details are also incomplete: the residual addition after the convolution is described in words but not written out, and the gating MLP is specified only as an "MLP-based gating network" without its depth, hidden sizes, activations, or the output projection required for the element-wise product to be dimensionally valid. Several symbol definitions and tensor shapes -- including exactly how L-Context is concatenated with the original sequence -- are unclear, and there are minor notation inconsistencies (for instance the additive epsilon term in the denormalization versus its stated role as a stability constant), small typos, and missing citations for borrowed concepts and metrics. On originality, the framing of lag as distinct from distribution shift, the portable latent-context mechanism, and the lag/tail metrics are the novel elements, whereas the individual building blocks (differencing, gating, recurrence, patching, relative position) are standard; the sensitivity study, moreover, varies only d_pos and leaves arguably more consequential hyperparameters (recurrent hidden size, patch size, convolution kernel) unexplored, which limits how firmly the specific design can be credited.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. The theoretical motivation is built on a recursive rollout (Eq. 2) in which each prediction feeds the next step, yet the baselines produce the full horizon in a single forward pass with no autoregressive feedback. How does the recursion apply to these models, and if their lag instead stems from post-switch samples gradually entering the fixed lookback window, how does L-Context specifically address that mechanism?
2. L-Context is cast as an estimator of the increment (Eq. 12), but the recurrent unit is trained only under the forecasting loss. Can you provide empirical evidence -- for example a correlation between L-Context outputs and ground-truth increments on held-out data -- that this interpretation holds, and clarify under what conditions the s_{t-1} ≈ y_hat_{t-1} approximation in the appendix is valid and when it breaks down?
3. Were the main and ablation results averaged over multiple seeds, and how many of the reported best results survive standard significance testing given margins at the 0.001 level? Would scale-normalized metrics such as MASE or MAPE materially change the comparison around switching points?
4. How sensitive are the lag metrics TailAUC and ExcessAUC to the percentile threshold and the neighborhood-window size, and could these choices be tuned to favor the proposed method? Why is CrossLinear excluded from the lag analysis in Table 4?
5. What role does the Struct-Aided Predictor play that is specific to change-aware forecasting rather than general overfitting reduction, and is there evidence that absolute-position memorization is actually a problem for the compared baselines? Please also clarify the details needed for reproduction: the concatenation of L-Context with the original sequence (axis and tensor shapes), the structure of the gating MLP, the choice of difference order, and whether gating the differenced signal risks amplifying high-frequency noise.
limitations: The limitations discussion is insufficient and would benefit from a dedicated section. In particular, the paper should acknowledge the mismatch between the recursive-rollout motivation and the single-forward-pass architectures actually evaluated, the fact that the interpretation of L-Context as an increment estimator is not enforced by the training objective, and the absence of variance and significance reporting behind performance and ablation margins that are often at the noise level.
